correctness.rationale: Most ETLs use natural-key upserts correctly and respect table PKs, but multiple pipelines serialize JSONB payloads with json.dumps before upsert, storing JSON as strings (not objects). This breaks raw_json querying and can silently impair downstream analysis. A few conflict-key choices in Garmin should be verified against the actual unique constraints to avoid non-matching ON CONFLICT. Backfill windows appear consistent and idempotent across sources.

robustness.rationale: Token refresh handling is solid (WHOOP/Spotify) and workflows prevent overlap via concurrency groups. However, HTTP fetches across Eight Sleep/WHOOP/Garmin lack retries/backoff for 5xx/timeouts beyond a few spots (401/429), risking silent partials. IMAP flows are resilient, marking as read only on success. sync_log logging exists widely, but several scripts omit sync_end/sync_start leading to inconsistent status visibility.

scalability.rationale: Pagination and HWM patterns are used (Spotify), and batching is generally reasonable. WHOOP and Spotify artist enrichment are rate-limit-aware. One notable inefficiency: Eight Sleep fetches all intervals every run and only filters client-side, which can scale poorly as history grows. ReccoBeats enrichment does a two-step per chunk that could be reduced.

idiomaticness.rationale: Clear separation of fetch/parse/write, consistent upsert helpers, and appropriate use of Supabase client. Good use of triggers and post-ETL maintenance steps. Minor inconsistencies (e.g., double-encoding JSONB, schema-qualified RPC usage) and limited retry logic prevent a 5.

response.summary:
The bundle is well-structured and largely idempotent: most tables are upserted on their natural keys, pagination is implemented where applicable, and workflows gate concurrency to prevent overlaps. Spotify shows exemplary patterns: high-water mark, separate heartbeats for enrichment subsystems, and robust token refresh. WHOOP and Garmin respect key dependencies (e.g., post-run TZ backfills), and the WHOOP journal path is carefully deduped with triggers backfilling behaviors_date if cycles arrive later.

The highest-impact issue is JSONB misuse: several ETLs json.dumps raw payloads before upserting into JSONB columns, resulting in JSON strings rather than objects. This breaks raw_json querying and could degrade downstream analysis or reparse logic. Retry/backoff is also thin across Eight Sleep/WHOOP/Garmin for transient 5xx/timeout cases. A smaller but consequential inconsistency: some sync_log entries omit sync_start/sync_end fields, harming freshness diagnostics.

Top 3 fixes:
1) Stop double-encoding JSONB across Garmin/WHOOP/Eight Sleep/MFP import; write dicts/lists directly. Provide a one-time migration to CAST stringified JSON into JSONB objects in-place.
2) Add resilient HTTP retry with exponential backoff and jitter for transient errors (5xx/timeouts) across Eight Sleep/WHOOP/Garmin clients; keep 401/429 handling as-is.
3) Standardize sync_log across scripts to always include sync_start and sync_end heartbeats (even on no-op) and schema-qualify RPC calls. Address Eight Sleep intervals overfetch by adding date filters or a rolling-window fetch.

findings.title: JSONB raw_json is double-encoded (string) in Garmin ETL
findings.description: Rows upserted into Garmin tables set raw_json via json.dumps(...). For JSONB columns this stores a JSON string, not an object, breaking raw_json-> queries and downstream processing.
findings.evidence: In sync_daily_summary row: "raw_json": json.dumps(stats). Similar patterns in sleep (json.dumps(sleep)), activities (json.dumps(act)), laps (json.dumps(lap)), workouts segment_targets json.dumps(...).
findings.recommendation: Pass native Python dict/list to Supabase so PostgREST stores JSONB objects. Remove json.dumps for JSONB columns. Run a one-time SQL migration to CAST existing stringified JSON into proper JSONB: UPDATE … SET raw_json = raw_json::jsonb WHERE jsonb_typeof(raw_json) IS NULL;

findings.title: WHOOP ETL stores JSONB as strings via json.dumps
findings.description: sync_cycles/sync_recovery/sync_sleep/sync_workouts use json.dumps for raw_json fields. This inserts string values into JSONB columns, impairing JSON operations.
findings.evidence: E.g., sync_cycles rows: "raw_json": json.dumps(c). Similar in recovery (r), sleep (s), workouts (w).
findings.recommendation: Remove json.dumps and send dicts directly. Backfill existing rows with ALTER TABLE + UPDATE casting raw_json::jsonb where needed.

findings.title: Eight Sleep ETL json.dumps raw_json into JSONB
findings.description: Upsert rows set "raw_json": json.dumps(_strip_timeseries(raw)), which stores JSON strings instead of JSONB objects.
findings.evidence: rows.append(row) block in sync_user sets raw_json via json.dumps.
findings.recommendation: Send raw dict; remove json.dumps. Backfill existing rows by casting text to JSONB.

findings.title: MyFitnessPal importer stores meals_json/raw_json as strings
findings.description: meals_json and raw_json are json.dumps-ed before upsert into JSONB columns, producing JSON strings.
findings.recommendation: Insert dicts directly (meals_json as dict, raw_json as dict). Add a migration to coerce existing rows to JSONB objects.

findings.title: Thin retry/backoff for transient HTTP failures (5xx/timeouts)
findings.description: Eight Sleep/WHOOP/Garmin clients mostly lack retries on 5xx or network timeouts. WHOOP handles 401/429 but not general 5xx; Eight Sleep has none. This risks silent partials when providers transiently fail.
findings.evidence: EightSleepClient.get_trends/get_intervals authenticate via httpx without retry. WhoopClient._request only refreshes on 401 and sleeps on 429; other 5xx are raised and skipped. Garmin per-call try/except logs and continues without retry.
findings.recommendation: Introduce a shared retry wrapper with exponential backoff + jitter for idempotent GETs (httpx/requests). Retry on 5xx/timeout up to N attempts; keep 401 refresh/429-reset logic. Log partials to sync_log with error details.

findings.title: Eight Sleep intervals overfetch on every run (no server-side date filter)
findings.description: get_intervals() fetches all intervals each run, then a client-side filter keeps only the target window. As history grows, this becomes increasingly wasteful.
findings.evidence: sync_user: intervals = client.get_intervals(user_id); then interval_by_date[...] limited to date window. No API params reduce server payload.
findings.recommendation: If API supports it, add from/to/date-range params to intervals. Otherwise, cache a max seen ts/date and request only recent intervals, or skip intervals entirely when trend v2 has the needed metrics for the window.

findings.title: Inconsistent sync_log heartbeats (missing sync_start/sync_end)
findings.description: Some ETLs omit sync_start/sync_end fields, hurting freshness visibility. journal_etl logs only sync_start; whoop_journal_email and myfitnesspal_email omit sync_start/end entirely.
findings.evidence: journal_etl.log_sync sets sync_start, no sync_end; myfitnesspal_email.log_sync and whoop_journal_email.log_sync don’t include sync_start/sync_end.
findings.recommendation: Standardize a shared log_sync utility to always set sync_start and sync_end. Emit a heartbeat even on 0-record runs to keep /status consistent.

findings.title: Schema-qualified RPC inconsistency for materialized view refresh
findings.description: eight_sleep_etl calls sb.rpc('refresh_materialized_views') without schema, while garmin/whoop use sb.schema('pds').rpc(...). If the function lives under pds, the unqualified call will fail.
findings.evidence: eight_sleep_etl: sb.rpc('refresh_materialized_views', {}). Other scripts: garmin_etl uses sb.schema('pds').rpc('refresh_materialized_views').
findings.recommendation: Use sb.schema('pds').rpc('refresh_materialized_views') consistently or move the function to public. Log and surface failures in sync_log.

findings.title: Verify Garmin ON CONFLICT targets match actual unique constraints
findings.description: Upserts use conflict keys like 'calendar_date,ts' and 'activity_id,ts'. If the DB unique index is only on calendar_date or activity_id, ON CONFLICT won’t match and can create duplicates (or fail at insert).
findings.evidence: Examples: upsert_to_supabase(... 'garmin_daily_summary', ..., 'calendar_date,ts'); upsert garmin_activities on 'activity_id,ts'; laps on 'activity_id,lap_index,ts'. Garmin schema is not in this bundle to confirm.
findings.recommendation: Cross-check conflict targets against actual unique indexes. Align the conflict column set to the table’s PK/unique constraint (e.g., activity_id if that’s the PK). Add a CI smoke test to reject mismatched on_conflict specs.

findings.title: ReccoBeats enrichment uses 1+N requests per chunk (avoidable)
findings.description: The ETL calls /track with batched IDs, then for each item calls /track/{id}/audio-features, doubling request count. This scales poorly if many tracks arrive in a burst.
findings.evidence: fetch_audio_features_reccobeats: GET /track?ids=... returns content with ReccoBeats ids, then loops per item to GET /track/{rb_id}/audio-features.
findings.recommendation: Prefer a single batch features endpoint if available, or parallelize with bounded concurrency and backoff. Cache resolved RB ids to avoid re-resolving for repeats.

findings.title: Eight Sleep trend parsing may miss/rename nested score fields
findings.description: safe_get paths like sleepDurationSeconds.score from sleepQualityScore/routine may not match actual v2 payload field names, risking NULL scores silently.
findings.evidence: parse_trend_day: 'sleep_duration_score': safe_get(quality, 'sleepDurationSeconds', 'score'); check official v2 schema for exact path names.
findings.recommendation: Log when expected score fields are missing and add unit tests with representative v2 payloads. Consider storing the source sub-objects (as JSONB) alongside parsed scalars to enable post-hoc fixes.

findings.title: WHOOP artists/body measurements minor style and consistency nits
findings.description: A few unused imports (hashlib), inconsistent spacing, and repeated json.dumps patterns reduce clarity.
findings.evidence: whoop_etl imports hashlib but does not use it; repeated json.dumps across sync_* functions (also a correctness defect captured in F-002).
findings.recommendation: Prune unused imports, DRY shared helpers, and adopt a shared JSONB upsert utility to prevent double-encoding.

things_done_well: Consistent upsert-on-natural-key design across ETLs (WHOOP: cycle_id/sleep_id/workout_id; Spotify: played_at+track_id; Eight Sleep: calendar_date+bed_side).
Spotify ETL shows robust patterns: high-water mark fetch, three separate sync_log heartbeats (plays, ReccoBeats, MusicBrainz), and token refresh with rotation persistence.
WHOOP journal path is thoughtfully idempotent: CSV importer dedupes on (cycle_date, question), IMAP flow marks read on success only, and DB triggers backfill behaviors_date when cycles land later.
Garmin/WHOOP workflows prevent overlapping runs via concurrency groups; token persistence via ci_token_helper is clean and per-service isolated.
TZ/backfill orchestration: GPS TZ backfill invoked post-Garmin; WHOOP TZ backfill invoked post-WHOOP — reduces cross-source ordering hazards.
Journal ETL uses edit-time guard and batch embeddings with a reputable model; vector dim matches schema and chunking is conservative.
questions_for_followup: Confirm the exact unique/primary key definitions for garmin_* tables (daily_summary, activities, laps, workouts). Should ON CONFLICT target only activity_id (activities) and calendar_date (daily_summary), or do composite uniques exist as coded?

Are raw_json and meals_json columns declared JSONB in all affected tables (Garmin, WHOOP, MFP, Eight Sleep)? If yes, can we run a migration to coerce existing stringified JSON into proper JSONB (and add a guard test)?

Eight Sleep intervals API: is there a server-side date-range filter we can leverage? If not, what is the typical intervals payload size for the account, and should we introduce a rolling max(date) cache to avoid repeated full pulls?
For sync_log standardization: do we want every subsystem to include both sync_start and sync_end, and to log on strict no-op runs (0 records) for heartbeat visibility?
ReccoBeats API: is there a batch audio-features endpoint to avoid the current 1+N pattern? If not, what QPS limits apply so we can choose an optimal parallelization strategy?
Materialized views RPC: is refresh_materialized_views installed under schema pds or public? Should all ETLs schema-qualify the RPC call to avoid environment drift?
WHOOP pagination: please confirm the response field name next_token and request param nextToken match the current API spec to future-proof the client (names appear mixed case by design).
